posts.title: Pillars of Creation, Eagle Nebula (Near-Infrared)
posts.text:
You're looking at columns of gas 5 light-years tall. For scale, our entire solar system would be a speck near the base of the left one.

This is the near-infrared version of Hubble's most famous image. Visible light can't get through the dust. Infrared can. Those bright points near the tops? Baby stars, invisible in the original 1995 photo.

The bluish haze along the edges is gas being boiled off by ultraviolet radiation from a cluster of massive young stars above the frame. The pillars are being destroyed in real time. In about 100,000 years, they'll be gone.

The original Pillars of Creation photo was taken on April 1, 1995. Not a joke. Probably the most reproduced astronomical image in history.

Image: NASA/ESA, Hubble Heritage Team (STScI/AURA), 2015.

posts.title: Cassiopeia A Supernova Remnant (Multi-Wavelength Composite)
posts.text:
A star exploded 11,000 years ago. It was 10,000 light-years away. This is what's left.

Cassiopeia A. Five different telescopes pointed at the same dead star. Gamma rays in magenta (Fermi). X-rays in blue and green (Chandra). Visible light in yellow (Hubble). Infrared in red (Spitzer). Radio waves in orange (Very Large Array).

The explosion was so powerful it's still accelerating particles to nearly the speed of light. Those particles, cosmic rays, are hitting Earth right now. They've been hitting Earth since before agriculture.

The star that made this was about 15 to 25 times the mass of our sun. Now it's a neutron star roughly 12 miles across.

Image: NASA/DOE/Fermi LAT Collaboration, CXC/SAO/JPL-Caltech, NRAO/AUI, 2017.

posts.title: Carina Nebula Pillar (Hubble 20th Anniversary)
posts.artist: NASA/ESA, M. Livio, Hubble 20th Anniversary Team (STScI)
posts.text:
Three light-years of gas and dust being eaten alive by the stars it created.

This pillar in the Carina Nebula is a stellar nursery. The bright spots near the top are infant stars, maybe a few hundred thousand years old. They're firing jets of gas outward like arrows.

But the stars forming nearby are also killing the pillar. Their ultraviolet radiation is burning the surface away. The column is shrinking. In a few million years it won't exist.

Hubble took this on its 20th birthday, April 2010. The telescope was launched on April 24, 1990, on the Space Shuttle Discovery. It was supposed to last 15 years.

It's still working.

Image: NASA/ESA, M. Livio, Hubble 20th Anniversary Team (STScI), 2010.
